what_it_does: This file is a noticeboard item that raises a design risk about address-based anchors outliving the code/design content they point to, and argues for a multi-selector, content-hashed, read-time re-anchoring strategy with loud failure.
observations: Identifies 'the anchor outlives the thing it points at' as the single biggest unsolved risk.
Contrasts 'mark-is-truth' immutability with the need to recompute anchor resolution at read time.
Cites Google Docs orphaned comments, Figma coordinate-vs-object anchoring limits, and Hypothesis fuzzy anchoring as category evidence.
Proposes a redundant bundle of selectors: block address, text quote, prefix, suffix, position offset, and content hash.
Recommends reusing the existing content-signature/freshness concept from dragnet cards for envelope staleness detection.
Advocates loud failure ('this annotation no longer matches its target') instead of silent mis-anchoring.
Notes that UI-mode elements are nested nodes inside a re-rendering tree, making them especially prone to drift.
standouts: The framing that storing a resolved DOM range is the Google Docs mistake and must be avoided.
The explicit split of 'mark-is-truth' into immutable observation versus dynamically recomputed anchor resolution.
The direct import of the W3C Web Annotation/Hypothesis fallback-selector cascade into this ledger's design.
conventions: Follows the noticeboard item YAML frontmatter schema with id, address, type, source, state, title, author_session, channel, links, timestamps, and history.
Uses markdown headings, bold emphasis, bullet lists, and inline external hyperlinks for citations.
Written as an argumentative design brief rather than a neutral status update.
concerns: The title in the frontmatter is truncated ('po').
Relies on external links for core evidence, which may rot.
Proposes a mechanism but does not specify the exact selector schema, reconciliation algorithm, or loud-failure UX.
Uses system-specific shorthand ('ui://', 'code://', 'ADDITIONS item the breakdown asked for') without defining them locally.
notes: A design-risk note arguing that content anchors must be multi-selector, content-hashed, and recomputed at read time to avoid silent mis-anchoring.
questions: What is the exact envelope schema for the proposed multi-selector anchor?
How is the content_hash computed and compared for a given block?
What does loud failure look like in the member/agent UX?
What is the relationship to the linked parent item board://item-64466290?
How would this apply specifically to ui:// element identities in a re-rendering tree?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the title frames the anchor-outlives-target risk, and the body extends that into a concrete multi-selector anchoring proposal.
apparent_themes: Robust anchoring through redundancy and fallback
Immutability of observations versus dynamic resolution of references
Learning from prior art in document annotation systems
intention_signals: It is trying to persuade the team to abandon frozen single-point anchors in favor of redundant, content-versioned selectors that are resolved at read time and fail loudly when stale.
novelty: It imports the W3C Web Annotation/Hypothesis fuzzy-anchoring model into a code/design substrate ledger and explicitly splits 'mark-is-truth' into immutable captured observation and mutable anchor resolution.
contribution: The insight that an envelope should carry a multi-selector anchor bundle tied to a content hash, and that anchor resolution should be recomputed at read time with detectable staleness and loud failure rather than stored as a frozen pointer.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard design note arguing that the ledger's block/element addressing risks anchors outliving edited targets, and proposing multi-selector, content-hashed, read-time re-anchoring with loud failure, drawing on Google Docs, Figma, and Hypothesis.
intention_for_embedding: To convince the team to replace frozen single-point anchors with redundant, version-aware selectors that are resolved dynamically and fail loudly when stale.
